_Captured Wednesday, 16 June 2021 12:44 PM · Computational › LUSAS_

- Rigid link for vertical offset;
- To model the required vertical offset
- ,
- I would generally recommend the use of a rigid, mass-less thick beam (with say EI x 1000 compared to the connected structural elements and some nominal sectional properties) instead of using rigid link constraints.
- To start with you could just create a new immaterial with E x 1000 compared to concrete/steel and use one of the actual geometric line attributes for sectional properties.
- Constraint equations add complexity which can be an issue in larger models, so using the simplest approach possible is preferred.
- As per point 2) I would recommend thick beams are used at all connection points but please check appropriate mesh, geometric and material attributes have been assigned

**Diagrams**
![Direct Method Influenc](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_165_direct-method-influenc_1.png)

- Load locations
- Search area
- - A
- search area
- can be used to limit the area over which the presecribed load is applied so that the effect of the load on other features in the model may prevented. If a search area is not specified the default loading area is
- Whole model
- .
- Nodes in search area
- - restricts the application of the applied load to just those mesh nodes within the search area, or to the whole model, as appropriate.
- For shell/plate/grillage models
- , it is recommended that the DMI analysis should be defined with the
- ‘Nodes in the search area’
- selected. For line beam models
- where the geometric beam section represents a loadable top slab
- , it is recommended that the
- Grid option be used
- instead, specifying Grid settings to automatically create grid points to match the longitudinal positions of the nodes in the search area.
- Grid
- - predominantly used with line beam models where the geometric beam section represents a loadable top slab. For this, the grid of points is used to represent the loadable region of the slab. The Grid method also provides the means to define a grid of points that can be used as an alternative to the nodes or points present in a she

**Diagrams**
![Search area](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_166_search-area_1.jpeg)
![Search area](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_166_search-area_2.jpeg)

![Search area](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_166_search-area_4.jpeg)

- Dear Sattar,
- Thanks for your email.
- You can inspect if the search area has been created properly as shown below.
- The area should be shown as a solid green. It seems that the number of the default maximum edges in the closed bays has been exceeded. You can increase this limit, but in this case it is created by the large number of mesh elements chosen per line (spacing = 0.5m). For a grillage model it would be expected that 1 division exist per line. I have changed the mesh as shown below and now the search area is generated.
- If you re-run the influence analysis and the VLO it should generate the load correctly. As a suggestion, to reduce the number of positions tried in the VLO, I would suggest that for this particular bridge you can make the spacing a bit coarser as shown below.
- I hope this helps.
- Best regards,
- Jesus

- Hi Sattar,
- Thanks for your query and the model file.
- As you wish to model cable catenary between two posts, bar elements are not appropriate as they are unable to bend.
- Instead, please use thick beams with an appropriate number of mesh divisions:
- If you intend the 2.5kN prestress force to be applied as the first loadcase, please modify the Stress Attribute:
- Also – you will need Nonlinear Loadcase controls with geometric nonlinearity activated – the axial stiffness of the cable depends upon the sag of the cable but this in turn depends upon the tension force.
- Right-Click on a Loadcase > Controls > Nonlinear & Transient > Click on Nonlinear Analysis Options:
- Please use Automatic Nonlinear Loadcase Controls with default settings as you only need one loadcase – this will help convergence

_Captured Thursday, 23 April 2020 9:18 PM · Computational › LUSAS_

- Reference Documents;
- 1019_BucklingAnalyses
- Linear Eigenvalue Buckling Analysis
- Geometrically Nonlinear Analysis
- In the “Nonlinear analysis options” the “Total Lagrangian” and “Co-rotational” options are selected to enable the geometric nonlinearities in the shells and beams elements, respectively.
- Without these options, nonlinear buckling will be completely ignored.
- Full Nonlinear Analysis
- A full nonlinear analysis would incorporate both geometric and material nonlinearities (as well as boundary nonlinearity if this was significant to the behaviour of the structure).
- A small hardening slope is being used only for numerical reasons; a “zero” value could potentially lead to numerical instabilities and nonconvergence of the model.

_Captured Thursday, 23 April 2020 9:02 PM · Computational › LUSAS_

- Reference Documents;
- 1030_Negative_Eigenvalues
- How do I eliminate negative eigenvalues from an eigenvalue buckling analysis
- An eigenvalue buckling analysis calculates the linear buckling load factors.
- This means that if the applied loads are amplified by the given load factor then the structure buckles with a specific deformed shape (mode shape).
- It is important
- load factors are close to unity
- Generally when negative eigenvalues are calculated and the points mentioned in paragraph 2.3 (and the web page) have been investigated, the negative eigenvalues can be ignored and the first positive eigenvalue could be considered as correct, provided that the error norm is small.
- How do I eliminate negative eigenvalues from an eigenvalue buckling analysis?
- It is essential to perform a linear static analysis prior to the eigenvalue analysis. This eliminates the added complexities of the eigen-analysis and enables a check on the basic stiffness matrix of the structure. Any warning or error messages in the LUSAS output file should be investigated. See the additional section on LUSAS Output Errors in this User area for more information
- Check the output file for any warnings or errors from the eigen-buckling analysis
- If using the
- Subspace eigensolver
- , increase the number of starting iteration vecto
